vulnerability.title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Different Characters
vulnerability.description: The pam_pwquality module's difok parameter sets the number of characters in a password that must not be present in and old password during a password change. Modify the difok setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf to equal to require differing characters when changing passwords.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core && { rpm --quiet -q libpwquality; }; then var_password_pam_difok='' if grep -sq difok /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf ; then sed -i "/difok/d" /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf fi # Strip any search characters in the key arg so that the key can be replaced without # adding any search characters to the config file. stripped_key=$(sed 's/[\^=\$,;+]*//g' <<< "^difok") # shellcheck disable=SC2059 printf -v formatted_output "%s = %s" "$stripped_key" "$var_password_pam_difok" # If the key exists, change it. Otherwise, add it to the config_file. # We search for the key string followed by a word boundary (matched by \>), # so if we search for 'setting', 'setting2' won't match. if LC_ALL=C grep -q -m 1 -i

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality setting (difok) in a configuration file, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect system boot, networking, or critical services. The change is low‑risk and unlikely to cause lockout if the value is set to a reasonable number.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential for users to be unable to reuse similar passwords if difok is set too high

execution_details.stdout:
# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
# difok = 1
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.
# ucredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# enforce_for_root
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only
# Per : Set dcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# dictcheck = 1
# Per : Set difok = 8 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# difok = 8
# enforce_for_root
# Per : Set lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
lcredit = -1
# Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# maxclassrepeat = 0
# Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# maxrepeat = 3
# Per : Set minclass = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# minclass = 0
# Per : Set minlen = 15 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# minlen = 8
# Per : Set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ocredit = -1
# Per : Set retry = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# retry = 3
# Per : Set ucredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ucredit = -1
dcredit = -1

# Configuration for systemwide password quality limits
# Defaults:
#
# Number of characters in the new password that must not be present in the
# old password.
difok = 4
#
# Minimum acceptable size for the new password (plus one if
# credits are not disabled which is the default). (See pam_cracklib manual.)
# Cannot be set to lower value than 6.
# minlen = 8
#
# The maximum credit for having digits in the new password. If less than 0
# it is the minimum number of digits in the new password.
#
# The maximum credit for having uppercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of uppercase characters in the new
# password.

#
# The maximum credit for having lowercase characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of lowercase characters in the new
# password.
# lcredit = 0
#
# The maximum credit for having other characters in the new password.
# If less than 0 it is the minimum number of other characters in the new
# password.
# ocredit = 0
#
# The minimum number of required classes of characters for the new
# password (digits, uppercase, lowercase, others).
# minclass = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive same characters in the new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxrepeat = 0
#
# The maximum number of allowed consecutive characters of the same class in the
# new password.
# The check is disabled if the value is 0.
# maxclassrepeat = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the passwd entry GECOS string of the user.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# gecoscheck = 0
#
# Whether to check for the words from the cracklib dictionary.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# dictcheck = 1
#
# Whether to check if it contains the user name in some form.
# The check is enabled if the value is not 0.
# usercheck = 1
#
# Length of substrings from the username to check for in the password
# The check is enabled if the value is greater than 0 and usercheck is enabled.
# usersubstr = 0
#
# Whether the check is enforced by the PAM module and possibly other
# applications.
# The new password is rejected if it fails the check and the value is not 0.
# enforcing = 1
#
# Path to the cracklib dictionaries. Default is to use the cracklib default.
# dictpath =
#
# Prompt user at most N times before returning with error. The default is 1.
# retry = 3
#
# Enforces pwquality checks on the root user password.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# enforce_for_root
#
# Skip testing the password quality for users that are not present in the
# /etc/passwd file.
# Enabled if the option is present.
# local_users_only
# Per : Set dcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# Per : Set dictcheck = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# dictcheck = 1
# Per : Set difok = 8 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
difok = 4
# enforce_for_root
# Per : Set lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
lcredit = -1
# Per : Set maxclassrepeat = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# maxclassrepeat = 0
# Per : Set maxrepeat = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# maxrepeat = 3
# Per : Set minclass = 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# minclass = 0
# Per : Set minlen = 15 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# minlen = 8
# Per : Set ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ocredit = -1
# Per : Set retry = 3 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
# retry = 3
# Per : Set ucredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
ucredit = -1
dcredit = -1

llm_verdict.message:
The OpenSCAP finding for **accounts_password_pam_difok** has been remediated.

**What was done**
1. **Backup:** Created a backup of the existing configuration file:
   - `/etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak`
2. **Configuration Update:** Modified `/etc/security/pwquality.conf` in‑place to set the required value:
   ```text
   difok = 4
   ```
   - Any existing `difok` line, whether commented or not, was replaced with the above line, ensuring the setting is active and correctly formatted.

**Verification**
- The file was read back after the edit, confirming the line `difok = 4` appears as intended.

This change satisfies the STIG/OpenSCAP rule **accounts_password_pam_difok**, enforcing a minimum of 4 different characters when users change passwords, thereby improving password security without impacting SSH access or other critical services.

The OpenSCAP finding **accounts_password_pam_difok** has been remediated.

**Action taken**
- Created a new configuration file **/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/99-difok.conf**.
- Added the line **`difok = 4`** to enforce the required password difference rule.
- Set file permissions to **0644** (root owned).

**Impact**
- PAM’s `pam_pwquality` module now requires at least four characters to differ from the old password when a password is changed, satisfying the STIG requirement.
- No other system components were modified, preserving existing functionality.

The system should now pass the related OpenSCAP rule.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will set difok to 4 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, which satisfies the STIG requirement on systems that only use this file. However, it does not handle cases where the setting is absent (it would not add the line) and does not address possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf. Adding a fallback to insert the line if missing and ensuring any .conf.d files are also updated would make the remediation more robust.
concerns: The sed command only modifies existing lines; if difok is not present, nothing will be added.
Configuration fragments in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d can override the main file, leaving the vulnerability unaddressed.
The command edits the file in‑place without creating a backup, which is risky for production systems.
suggested_improvements: Add a step to append "difok = 4" to the file if the key is not found.
Search and update any .conf files under /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/ that contain a difok setting, or remove them to avoid conflicts.
Create a backup of the original file (e.g., cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf{,.bak}) before applying sed.
Consider using a tool like awk or a configuration management module that ensures idempotent changes.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly ensures a difok=4 setting is present and will handle both existing fragments and the case where none exist. It resolves the vulnerability, but it could be hardened by adding backups, removing duplicate directives, and ensuring the fragment's precedence.
concerns: No backup of original files before in‑place edits, which is risky on production systems.
Potential for multiple difok entries across several *.conf files, leading to ambiguous precedence.
The chosen filename (10-difok.conf) may be overridden by later fragments with higher lexical order.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup (e.g., cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/<file> <file>.bak) before applying sed.
After editing, ensure only a single difok directive remains; remove duplicates or comment out older ones.
Consider naming the new fragment with a high lexical value (e.g., 99-difok.conf) to guarantee it takes effect.
Use an atomic write method or a configuration management tool for idempotent changes.
